speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six, Bruce Cohen interviewing Halina Nelken, November 24, 1995. OK. As far as we were talking about the short period of time you stayed in Plaszow between approximately December 1943 and January, February 1944, Is there anything-- given the short time, is there anything else you can add to this particular experience with respect to describing the camp or its activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very short time the first time, when I was in Plaszow. So I would rather come back to the other one, when-- when I was again and longer. But the life of the Plaszow and the rhythm of Plaszow was exactly the same. There was about five o'clock well, call for getting out. And it was played on the trumpet. And it-- words were terrible, dirty . And we were being-- we had to go to the roll call. And all the time, till they finally-- well, seeing that everybody's there and so forth, then we have to go to work. And then back in the evening, all the time it was actually roll call. Roll call, roll call. And I was working then in the mattress factory. And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still during the '43--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is still this first time, yeah. And I was working at night shift. So I was working at night, which was terrible. I couldn't stand it. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't stand it because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because lack of sleep. I was too tired physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many hours were you working during the day then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, during the day, we were not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Total-- lack of sleep. Meaning, how much sleep did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that's when it was about 1 o'clock in the morning, I-- I was falling asleep. And I-- I was like working in a sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would get up, you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: At 6:00, at the whole night, we were working. And then at 6 o'clock to roll call, and then eventually about 9 o'clock in the morning, we were in the barracks. And there, already my mother was working. I haven't seen her even. And then in the evening, about 6 o'clock, again-- so this was terrible. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now, let's get-- the family members that were with you. You continued to stay with the same family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I continued to stay with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all transported together. Your mother, your father, and your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my mother, yeah. And my brother already was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's your brother? He's here also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was there also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. So then after this two or three months stay there, you went to which camp? You were transported to which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was transported to that Nachrichtemgerratenlager. This is very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-A-- this takes five minutes. N-A-C-H-R-I-C-H-T-E-N-G-E-R-A-T-E-N-L-A-G-E-R. One word.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what type of camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was like a magazine for communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: A magazine for communication meaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if they wanted telephone poles and telephone-- those things reach out, which hang on telephone poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a telecommunication--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- and all the time we had to-- they carried a big train full of this. We had to unload it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Full of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of this. Of this. These telephone poles, and see, I don't know how you  call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In German, it's [GERMAN] They say. And we had two--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what is across the telephone pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: A line?

speaker: interviewee
text: A line and whatever. We had to unload this. And then when we had to load again, and it was going to the east front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were here from February 1944. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime in the spring. I think it was the end of May or beginning of June 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the-- again, the-- so was this also a type-- what-- when we speak of type of camp, now, would you call it a forced labor camp? Is that fair to say, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a forced labor camp, which belonged to Plaszow also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was under the administration of Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the administration of Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where was this place located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was located on the Vistula River, just across from Schindler's enamel factory

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, what was life like here? Could you characterize a day in the life of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- I went there because I thought that my papers were ready. And I had these papers to run away. And since this was practically in the city, it was just enough to go through the door, through the entrance door, and be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean  papers to run away? When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had still in-- on that fliegerhorst, I got my false papers, which were actually even right papers, from my Polish friend who saved my diaries through a civilian German with whom I was working in-- at the fliegerhorst. At this-- at this office for to exchange the parts to the airplanes. I told you that I was there for the clerical job. And he was an older civilian. And he wanted-- he and his wife wrote to me, even they wanted to take me to save me, in other words. So he went to my Polish friend, brought all my dresses, and brought all my diaries, and everything, and wanted me-- wanted me to-- you know, to run away. And I was ready. On one day, I was supposed to run away from the fliegerhorst. And I let my parents know about it and everything. And then let's say I was supposed to run away on Thursday. And on Tuesday, suddenly, the whole camp on fliegerhorst was evacuated to Plaszow. So Hitler was much more quicker than we were. And I-- but I still had my papers there. And she wrote me that my Polish friend said, let me know, because people were working outside of Plaszow. That was-- were coming there. That as soon I would be out of Plaszow, it's still not too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I found myself in the nachrichtengeratenlager, I thought this is just perfect. I don't know anyone here. I don't have any bonds. And so many people run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will run away too. And especially this guy at the gate was-- knew me, because he was our neighbor from the street where I lived before the war. And he was this one who brought a letter to me from my Polish friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought OK. Here, I will run away. But then, the very first day when I arrived, I didn't want to talk to anyone. I didn't want to get close to anyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to have that feeling that I am leaving someone to be responsible for my running away. I knew that who knows what is going to happen to them. But as long as I don't know them and nothing, I don't feel so bad. But then, it ate my heart, this whole thing. And I was working so hard this very first day when I arrived. And I felt so bad looking at these people, when one of these women came and put her arms around me and said, please, child, don't worry. It's not so bad. It will be better. You know, I pushed her away. I didn't want it, even, because I knew right away that because she showed me so much goodness and so much-- such a cordiality, and she was so good to me that now I am unable to run away, because this is already a person whose goodness of heart stands against me and my freedom. And I pushed her away. And I started to cry so badly that I thought that really, my heart was falling apart. Well, but she was really saying, no, well, everything will be better. Better. And I knew. I did not run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could actually get papers to get out of a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: People could get out of concentration camps with papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- with papers, people can get out of everything. I already had mine. But this was the way how you will get out. Well, so I didn't do it. And then, this was the irony. That later on, while there were three other young girls, very nice, working there in the same barrack. And it was a nice evening, spring evening. May. It was perhaps the first of May or so. And then we were talking about this. And they knew at this time that I had a possibility to run away. I had papers and so forth. And they asked me, because I read to them a poem about freedom that I wrote there. Well. And well, it was like this. That freedom is calling me, and where it's so beautiful, and the moon is there, and spring smells beautifully from the Vistula River.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to be free. And I wanted to run away. And in such a moment, I see that freedom is calling me in vain. And freedom, why do you call me in vain? And in such a moment, so beautiful, everything is getting up and rising in me and dying at the same moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING  POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I read this poem to them. It has a very nice rhythm in the Polish language. And then all three of them look at me, Helena, if you had now this possibility as you had then, would you run away? I said, I don't know. Maybe I would. Oh, how could you? And that responsibility-- collective responsibility. You will be free. We will be dead. How could you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: All three  of them jumped on me. And they did not know that I have this paper all the time with me, but I did not run away. The next day, all three of them disappear. All three of them run away. All three of them also survived the war. But the SS came from Plaszow. And all of us who were there, about 60 people, we were ready to be executed. And we would be, if not that suddenly a whole three lots of trains arrived with an immediate rule and order to be unloaded and immediately sent to the East Front. And therefore, this saved our lives. We were just beaten, but we did not-- we were not executed. And we worked for two days, day and night, without food. But we were happy that they were not going to shoot us. However--

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, you knew. At that point, you knew all about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fate of many people. At what point did you first find out about all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: About that they survived? After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no, no, no. That-- that you could be sent off and exterminated.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, we knew from the very beginning of the war that there is no-- that our only hope it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, did you know about the extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I still did not know. I know this but well because of this. That they run away. We were taken back to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to know the names of those three girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: I might have them somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was one. And I know that they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this camp, you were still with your mother, father, and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In nachrichtengeratenlager, I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this time, my father already was sent to Auschwitz and executed. And gassed. But I did not know it. I just knew that he was sent on about between 13 or 15th of May, that he was sent to, as they said, matches factory in Birkenau. Nobody knew about Birkenau. People knew about Auschwitz, that there is  a camp. But we did not know what was going on. But we did not know about Birkenau. And in Birkenau were the gas chambers. So my father was not anymore there. But when  I came back to Plaszow, I was with my mother, and my brother was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So essentially, just in terms of as far as treatment went, before we get to Plaszow here, as far as treatment went between February '44 and June '44 at this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- well, this was too a small of camp to be, you know, so-- so-- but they were-- there was-- the head of this camp was such a small, short guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His  name was Krak, K-R-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And he was-- he showed up one day into our barrack in the evening. And I was in my, you know, [INAUDIBLE] in my bed, doing something. And the girls were all sitting around the table and eating the soup. And when he came-- so attention. Everybody should stay attention. So I stayed at attention too. But about three seconds later then everybody else, till I get out of this-- of this thing. And that Krak, when he noticed this, he grabbed a bowl of soup from the table and threw it at me. But he didn't get me. And one after another, he was taking the bowl with soup into my direction, and not one landed on me. And then he said, I have to go and see him tomorrow, next day. Next day when I came to his office, he wanted to, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hit my-- my cheek. But he was short. And he did not get-- he hit me here. So he let me go into the corner. He brought a little chair. He get on this chair, and then he started to beat me, and beat me terribly. Almost fall off this chair and hold it to my--

speaker: interviewer
text: He beat you with his hands? With an instrument?

speaker: interviewee
text: With his hands. With his hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were-- what did they feed you at this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some water soup with potatoes or whatever. You know. Hardly anyone was eating that soup. We were trying to do as much as we can to buy something outside through what was smuggled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you transported to this camp from Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were transported on the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: On trucks again. Trucks, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was not far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So essentially-- oh, right. So moving back to Plaszow then.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back to Plaszow, where first of all, I was-- I already had that experience. The first time in my life I was-- someone hit me on may face-- slapped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's just date this. This is-- you have arrived now back in Plaszow on what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about the end of May, beginning of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you transported to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, on trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, by truck, OK. And now you are with the rest of your family, other than your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was there too. But you see. You cannot see one another. You could see someone only for the short time between the roll call and-- and the curfew, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men and women were divided.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many people were at the camp before this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they have so many-- so many statistics. Plaszow is very well covered with statistics.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- could you describe your treatment? You were about to describe your treatment at Plaszow this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow at this time, I was working in that Kranken-Revier in this like cleaning or hospital-- small hospital for the SS men. And for that-- they were people from Latvian and Ukrainian who were in SS uniforms. Not SS uniforms, but who were there with the Germans. So there I was, just cleaning this-- I was the cleaning lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your specific duty, in other words.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in terms of the living conditions here, it's the same thing as you've described before?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are the same, yes, barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the clothes, those striped suits, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this time, I-- even before we went from the nachrichtengeratenlager, after this girl ran away, they brought us the striped jackets. The striped clothes. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the kind of food you were given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Plaszow, it was also this terrible soup and-- and this terrible, well, so to speak, coffee, what was water, and a piece of bread and sometimes margarine. Only once when there was a commission from the Red Cross, we got egg and something like sugar. And the whole camp was doing kogel-mogel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Red Cross think when they saw a camp like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You have to ask them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you portrayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I did not see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much sleep were you getting here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was regularly, well, about 10 o'clock till 5:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you essentially hungry, or were you full?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were not hungry, because when we were working, everybody who was working with-- well, like-- like I was in this Kranken-Revier, and then they always let-- there was always bread and all kinds of things. So we were two-- one woman, Madame [NON-ENGLISH], she was working as a secretary, and I was a cleaning lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any atrocities here, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- no. At this time, in the Revier, no. But there was. There was-- of course, there was that mountain of where they do the execution in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what-- for what-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were-- we-- it was very-- in full view from that roll call plat. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason would someone be executed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ha! What kind of a question is this? You never, during the Second World War, when you were Jewish, and when you were Polish, you never asked a German why do they execute people? Because they want to, period. This is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that some people would be able to-- you-- how could some people escape it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because obviously, at this time, they could not-- at-- at this time, they needed us badly in the camp. Because if not, they would go on the front to Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how minimal could an offense be for someone to be executed? Even a look or  a glance? Would that be sufficient?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, of course, glance or not glance, or because of this or because of that. Whatever you did or whatever you didn't. Sometimes we're-- say we're in Plaszow. As a matter of fact, the SS were absolutely you know, free. Anytime, they could go into the barrack or into the latrine or into this and that and shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there was one person that caused a problem, would others in the same barrack be punished as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. When there was-- when they found that someone had smuggled something, then they  shoot everybody. When they found-- it was not like this. This was-- It is very difficult to answer, because this question seems so naive to the reality that was there. I saw once when there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not-- I understand the-- I understand the reaction. The point is is to-- is to get an understanding of the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the lack of reason for it. The fact that it could be anything. It's a-- I understand--

speaker: interviewee
text: Any--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand the question as you perceive it. But I ask it to provide--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know why you ask, because you are a logical person, and you are trying desperately to find a logical reason between this, what had absolutely no logic.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am not even suggesting it's logical at all. I'm just not even looking for logic.

speaker: interviewee
text: No morality, nothing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just looking for what it would take. And what you're saying is nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just nothing. You see, a friend of mine who is still in Krakow, he was so young at this time. And the men were working outside and taking some difficult things and whatever. And then, they-- it was hard. So they took the jackets or sweaters and told him, you take care of this. Watch that nobody would steal it. And he was doing this. And Amon Goth, the commander, he was watching through the binoculars from his seat. And he noticed that there is someone sitting. And he brought his gun, and bang, to kill him. And this thing was in his head, but he was not killed. It stuck, the bullet, here. But he was wise enough-- well, first of all, he was frightened. So he was playing dead. After the war, he had operated this. But you see, he did not know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very quickly, before the end of the tape, were there many instances of smuggling going on regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what type of things? Food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, because people had to have food. And they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was helping? Who was getting it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people smuggled food for their families or for sale. And then there was-- for example, they had to bribe that postern, the German, so that he brought the commanders through there without looking for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to switch tapes.
